subtopics.subtopic_title: EPIDEMIOLOGY AND DOUBLE BURDEN MALNUTRITION IN INDIAN ADOLESCENTS
subtopics.concept:
Adolescence, defined as the age range of 10–19 years, is a critical period of nutritional vulnerability due to the simultaneous demands of linear growth, lean mass accumulation, bone mineralization, blood volume expansion, and, in girls, menarche-related iron losses. In India, the epidemiological profile of adolescent nutrition is characterized by a double burden of malnutrition, where undernutrition and micronutrient deficiencies coexist with rising cardiometabolic risks. National survey data, such as the Comprehensive National Nutrition Survey (CNNS) 2016–18, reveal that approximately one-quarter of Indian adolescents experience thinness (BMI-for-age <-2 SD), while overweight and obesity (BMI-for-age >+1 SD) are also prevalent. Micronutrient deficiencies are widespread, with anemia being particularly common. Girls exhibit higher rates of anemia and iron deficiency compared to boys, and deficiencies in vitamin D, folate, vitamin B12, and zinc are frequently observed, often in combination. Among older adolescents, biomarker data from NFHS-5 (2019–21) highlight that anemia affects a majority of girls aged 15–19 years and about one-third of boys in the same age group.

Several risk factors contribute to this nutritional burden, including poor diet quality influenced by increased autonomy, peer norms, academic pressures, aggressive marketing, and inadequate sleep. These factors often exacerbate the mismatch between nutritional needs and dietary intake during adolescence. Clinically, signs such as fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, poor academic performance, recurrent infections, menstrual irregularities, and stress-related injuries should be recognized as potential indicators of nutritional deficits rather than dismissed as non-specific complaints. Structured nutritional screening at every clinical encounter is strongly recommended, as normal weight does not preclude micronutrient deficiencies, and rapid changes in growth trajectories (e.g., crossing centiles) may be more clinically significant than isolated measurements.

Sanitation and prevention strategies for caregivers were not specified in the source. However, addressing the double burden of malnutrition in Indian adolescents requires a comprehensive approach that integrates regular nutritional assessments with targeted interventions to address both undernutrition and emerging risks of overnutrition.

subtopics.subtopic_title: NATIONAL PROGRAM LINKAGES FOR ADOLESCENT NUTRITION CARE IN INDIA
subtopics.concept: Adolescent nutrition care in India is closely integrated with national programs delivered through schools, community platforms, and adolescent-friendly health services. The Rashtriya Kishor Swasthya Karyakram (RKSK) framework prioritizes nutrition and provides a structured referral system for counseling, screening, and linking adolescents to supplements and deworming services under the National Health Mission (NHM). Weekly Iron and Folic Acid Supplementation (WIFS) is a key operational component, involving supervised weekly ingestion of iron-folic acid tablets alongside biannual deworming. Practical bedside decision-making should focus on verifying the delivery chain, ensuring adolescents are enrolled, receiving and ingesting the tablets, addressing adherence barriers such as side effects or myths, and confirming deworming completion. For undernourished or migrating adolescents, regular school meal access through the PM-POSHAN scheme is essential to enable counseling and nutritional support. Clinical management of anemia should align with standard treatment protocols, transitioning to prevention and maintenance through national programs once hemoglobin levels and symptoms improve. It is critical for paediatric trainees to consult the latest Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW) guidelines for accurate information on tablet composition, eligibility criteria, and thresholds, as operational details may evolve. Linking clinic care with school health platforms further enhances adherence and reduces follow-up losses, particularly for adolescents in hostels or government schools. At discharge, clinicians should explicitly map the treatment plan to existing delivery mechanisms rather than relying solely on family-based follow-up. For anemia management, severity, therapeutic course, and follow-up dates should be documented, with prevention transitioned to school-based WIFS after treatment completion. The Anemia Mukt Bharat approach—screening, treating, testing for response, and addressing underlying causes—should be utilized, alongside coordination with the National Deworming Day (NDD) schedule. In cases of heavy menstrual bleeding or bleeding disorders, red flags and referral pathways to gynecology or hematology should be documented, enabling school health teams to reinforce urgent follow-up if symptoms recur. For obesity and lifestyle risks, referrals to the School Health Programme under Ayushman Bharat should be initiated for continued care. Documenting program linkages, including WIFS adherence, deworming, and school meal access, ensures effective follow-up and prevents repeated treatment without delivery.

subtopics.subtopic_title: UNIFIED OUTPATIENT WORKFLOW FOR ADOLESCENT NUTRITION VISITS
subtopics.concept: A unified outpatient workflow for adolescent nutrition visits is essential due to the frequent overlap of growth concerns, menstrual symptoms, athletic training, and disordered eating in this age group. At every contact, a minimum screening protocol should be completed before initiating counseling. This includes plotting height, weight, and BMI on age-appropriate growth charts and interpreting the trajectory; documenting pubertal stage or menarche status; conducting a focused dietary recall covering the previous 24 hours and typical weekly patterns, including meals, breakfast skipping, consumption of milk or curd, ultraprocessed snacks, vegetarian or vegan status, and food insecurity. Additionally, quantify physical activity and training load by assessing frequency (days/week), duration (minutes/day), recent increases, heat exposure, and weight-cutting pressures. The menstrual cycle should be treated as a vital sign, with attention to cycle patterns, heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) such as flooding or clots, pad changes, dysmenorrhea, and school absenteeism. A brief psychosocial screen using the HEADSS framework should be completed, along with an eating disorder screener like SCOFF when indicated. Explicit questions regarding supplements and stimulants must also be included. Following screening, urgency should be triaged before ordering tests. Immediate referral or admission is required for medical instability, including severe bradycardia (HR <50/min daytime or <45/min nighttime), hypotension (BP <90/45 mm Hg), temperature <35.6°C, significant orthostatic instability (sustained HR rise >40/min or BP fall >20 systolic or >10 diastolic), syncope, seizures, electrolyte disturbances, prolonged QTc interval, acute food refusal, uncontrollable bingeing or purging, significant HMB, or exertional collapse/altered sensorium suggestive of heat illness. For medically stable patients, management should align with the dominant phenotype, such as growth spurt support, menstrual symptom pathway, athlete/RED-S pathway, or suspected eating disorder pathway. A resource-adapted baseline panel should be ordered to guide management, and explicit follow-up should be scheduled. Patients with recent weight loss, amenorrhea in athletes, moderate anemia with ongoing HMB, or suspected eating disorders require review within 3–7 days, as deferring review for months is unsafe.

subtopics.subtopic_title: STANDARDIZED ORTHOSTATIC VITAL SIGN MEASUREMENT AND THRESHOLDS
subtopics.concept: Standardized orthostatic vital sign measurement is a critical tool in paediatric practice, particularly in the context of conditions such as eating disorders, heavy menstrual bleeding with anemia, and heat illness. To ensure consistency, orthostatic vitals should be measured after 5 minutes of supine rest and again after 3 minutes of standing. At both time points, heart rate (HR) and blood pressure (BP) must be documented, along with any symptoms such as dizziness or presyncope. It is essential to ensure the adolescent has not recently engaged in physical exertion; if they have, the test should be delayed until after a quiet rest period. Concerning orthostatic changes are defined as a pulse increase of >20 beats per minute, a systolic BP drop of >20 mmHg, or a diastolic BP drop of >10 mmHg. These thresholds, when combined with other markers of medical instability—such as bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, dehydration, electrolyte disturbances, prolonged QTc, syncope, seizures, or acute food refusal—should prompt further clinical evaluation. While a pulse increase of >40 beats per minute may indicate a higher level of concern, it should not be used as a separate definition but rather as an additional flag within the same diagnostic framework. Contextual factors must be considered when interpreting orthostatic changes. Anxiety, pain, fever, dehydration, or recent exposure to stimulants or caffeine can exaggerate these changes, while beta-blockers and severe malnutrition may blunt compensatory tachycardia. In cases of uncertainty, symptoms should guide decision-making, and measurements should be repeated after hydration and rest. If orthostatic changes meet the defined thresholds and are accompanied by recent weight loss, restrictive intake, recurrent vomiting or purging, or significant anemia, same-day higher-level review or admission assessment is warranted. A minimum safety panel, including electrolytes (phosphate and magnesium), glucose, and an ECG, should be obtained if bradycardia, palpitations, syncope, or stimulant exposure is present. Follow-up visits should include repeat orthostatic measurements under standardized conditions to monitor recovery.

case_studies.title: Orthostatic Vital Signs in an Adolescent with Fatigue and Dizziness
case_studies.vignette: A 15-year-old girl presents to a rural clinic in India with complaints of fatigue, dizziness, and occasional fainting episodes over the past 2 weeks. She mentions heavy menstrual bleeding over the last two cycles and reduced appetite recently. On further questioning, she denies any recent physical exertion or stimulant use. On examination, she appears pale and mildly lethargic. Her supine vitals are HR 88 bpm and BP 110/70 mmHg after 5 minutes of rest. After standing for 3 minutes, her HR increases to 112 bpm, and her BP drops to 90/60 mmHg. She reports feeling lightheaded during the standing phase. No other acute findings are noted on examination. The clinic is equipped with basic resources, and the patient lives in a low-income household with limited access to specialty care.
case_studies.learning_objective: Interpret orthostatic vital sign changes and identify concerning thresholds for further evaluation in an adolescent presenting with fatigue and dizziness.

case_studies.title: Orthostatic Vital Signs in an Adolescent with Fatigue and Dizziness
case_studies.vignette: A 15-year-old girl presents to a rural health clinic in India with complaints of fatigue, dizziness upon standing, and difficulty concentrating over the past two weeks. She mentions heavy menstrual bleeding over the last three cycles and reduced appetite. Her mother reports that she has lost noticeable weight recently. On examination, she appears pale but is afebrile. After 5 minutes of supine rest, her blood pressure is 100/70 mmHg and heart rate is 82 beats per minute. After standing for 3 minutes, her blood pressure drops to 80/60 mmHg, and her heart rate increases to 110 beats per minute. She reports feeling lightheaded during the standing measurement. No other acute symptoms, such as syncope or seizures, are noted. The clinic has limited resources, but a point-of-care hemoglobin test reveals a level of 8 g/dL.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize and interpret concerning orthostatic vital sign changes in the context of adolescent nutrition and anemia, and apply thresholds to guide triage or further evaluation.

subtopics.subtopic_title: GROWTH SPURT PHYSIOLOGY AND PUBERTAL NUTRIENT COUNSELING
subtopics.concept: The adolescent growth spurt represents a critical period of rapid physiological changes, including heightened height velocity, lean mass gain, bone mineral accrual, and increased physiological turnover such as erythropoiesis. These processes significantly elevate nutrient demands, and inadequate energy availability or poor diet quality can manifest as fatigue, delayed recovery after physical activity, delayed pubertal progression, dysmenorrhea, or menstrual irregularities, often preceding visible signs of thinness. Menarche timing is clinically relevant; earlier menarche increases cumulative iron-loss exposure, while delayed menarche or halted pubertal progression may indicate undernutrition or excessive physical training. Nutrient counseling during this phase should be anchored to ICMR-NIN nutrient reference values, with explicit acknowledgment that these targets are based on reference age/sex body weights and vary with pubertal stage, growth velocity, and physical activity levels. Protein quality is particularly important, as cereal-based diets may necessitate intakes closer to ~1 g/kg/day compared to the adult mixed-diet RDA of 0.83 g/kg/day. For practical bedside counseling, focus on high-yield nutrient targets. Adolescents require approximately 1000 mg of calcium daily during mid-to-late adolescence, 600 IU/day of vitamin D when not deficient, and higher iron intake in post-menarcheal girls compared to boys. Nutritional advice should be delivered through actionable meal swaps rather than abstract nutrient lectures. For example, encourage breakfast and post-school meals such as poha or upma with peanuts and milk, idli or dosa with sambar and curd, or paratha with curd and egg, paneer, or sprouts. Calcium adequacy can be supported by incorporating milk, curd, paneer, and calcium-rich millets like ragi. To mitigate iron deficiency risks, pair iron-rich foods with vitamin C sources and advise against consuming tea or coffee close to iron-rich meals. These practical strategies help address the heightened nutritional needs during the adolescent growth spurt and support optimal pubertal development.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CLINIC-READY ASSESSMENT AND RESOURCE-ADAPTED BASELINE INVESTIGATIONS
subtopics.concept: Clinic-ready assessment and resource-adapted baseline investigations in adolescent nutrition require a structured, efficient, and trajectory-based approach. Dietary evaluation should incorporate a 24-hour recall and a "usual week" pattern to identify key nutritional gaps, including total calorie intake, protein quality and distribution, calcium and iron intake, inhibitors of iron absorption, high free-sugar beverage consumption, reliance on ultra-processed snacks, and restrictive eating behaviors. Clinicians must explicitly inquire about food insecurity, dieting practices, and, in athletes, intentional dehydration or rapid weight loss. Anthropometric measurements should use adolescent-appropriate references, such as the IAP 2015 growth charts for Indian clinics or the WHO 2007 reference for global standardization. The WHO defines thinness as BMI-for-age <-2 SD, overweight as >+1 SD, and obesity as >+2 SD. Blood pressure and waist circumference should be measured, as central adiposity may coexist with a normal BMI. Baseline investigations should prioritize tests that directly influence management and be tailored to severity and available resources. In stable adolescents, a pragmatic initial set includes a complete blood count (CBC) with indices, ferritin or iron studies for anemia, fatigue, or heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB), thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) for menstrual disorders, growth deceleration, or unexplained weight changes, and 25(OH)D for bone pain, fractures, or high-risk patterns of low sun exposure or low calcium intake. For adolescents aged 10 years and older with overweight or obesity, fasting lipids, glycemic screening (including HbA1c where locally applicable), and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) should be added to screen for metabolic-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD). In cases of heightened physiological risk, such as significant weight loss, purging, syncope, stimulant exposure, or suspected deficiencies, additional tests may include electrolytes (magnesium and phosphate), liver function tests (LFT), an electrocardiogram (ECG), and assessments for vitamin B12, folate, zinc, and pregnancy when indicated. Where follow-up is uncertain, bundling likely tests into a single blood draw is recommended to avoid missing coexisting deficiencies. This structured approach ensures comprehensive yet resource-sensitive care for adolescents presenting with nutritional concerns.

case_studies.vignette: A 14-year-old girl presents to a rural outpatient clinic in India with complaints of fatigue and difficulty concentrating over the past 3 months. Her mother reports irregular eating patterns, with frequent consumption of packaged snacks and sugary beverages due to school demands. She has heavy menstrual bleeding lasting 5–6 days per cycle for the past year, which her mother attributes to puberty. On focused examination, her BMI-for-age is -1.5 SD (IAP 2015 growth chart), and she appears pale. Waist circumference is within normal limits. Blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg. The clinician performs a dietary recall, noting low iron-rich food intake and high consumption of iron absorption inhibitors like tea. The family admits to occasional food insecurity. Baseline investigations include a complete blood count (CBC) showing hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL and mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 72 fL.

subtopics.subtopic_title: INTEGRATING CLINIC CARE WITH SCHOOL HEALTH PLATFORMS
subtopics.category: NATIONAL PROGRAM LINKAGES FOR ADOLESCENT NUTRITION CARE IN INDIA
subtopics.concept: Integrating clinic care with school health platforms is a vital strategy to improve adherence and reduce loss to follow-up in adolescent nutrition care, particularly for those in hostels or government schools. At the time of discharge from clinic visits, it is essential to explicitly map the care plan to existing delivery mechanisms rather than relying solely on family purchase or memory. For anemia management, clinicians should document the severity, therapeutic course, and follow-up date, transitioning to prevention through school-based Weekly Iron and Folic Acid Supplementation (WIFS) programs after treatment completion. The Anemia Mukt Bharat approach—comprising screening, treatment, testing for response, and addressing underlying causes—should be employed, with deworming coordinated through the National Deworming Day (NDD) schedule when appropriate. In cases of heavy menstrual bleeding or suspected bleeding disorders, red flags and referral pathways (e.g., gynecology or hematology) must be clearly documented so that school health teams can reinforce the need for urgent follow-up if symptoms recur. For obesity and lifestyle-related risks, the School Health Programme under Ayushman Bharat serves as the referral destination for ongoing counseling, activity promotion, and tracking. Comorbidity screenings ordered in the clinic should be completed and reviewed as part of this process. Collaboration with the school meal ecosystem, such as the PM-POSHAN (Mid-Day Meal) scheme, is critical. Practical guidance should focus on healthy food choices—avoiding sugar-sweetened beverages and prioritizing nutrient-dense options like pulses, eggs, and milk—rather than unrealistic meal replacement advice. For adolescent athletes and those at risk of Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S), clinicians should document training loads, identified risk markers, and a food-first plan. Families should be advised to avoid unregulated supplements, using medically indicated products only for proven deficiencies. Providing a one-page written plan with two to three measurable targets and a clear follow-up date is often the most effective linkage tool in Indian settings. This structured integration of clinic care with school health platforms ensures continuity of care and reinforces national program goals for adolescent nutrition.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ENERGY TARGETS AND FOOD-GROUP PORTION PRESCRIPTION
subtopics.category: DIET PRESCRIPTION IN CLINIC FOR ADOLESCENT GROWTH AND WEIGHT PHENOTYPES
subtopics.concept: For effective dietary prescription in adolescents to support growth and weight management, energy targets should be anchored to age, sex, and activity levels using ICMR-NIN reference tables, with adjustments based on growth velocity, training load, and adiposity phenotype. For adolescents with typical school activity, practical energy anchors are approximately 2860 kcal/day for boys aged 13–15 years and 2400 kcal/day for girls in the same age group, increasing to 3320 kcal/day for boys aged 16–18 years and 2500 kcal/day for girls in this older group. Adjustments of ±10–20% are recommended: increase energy intake during periods of peak height velocity, underweight status, or supervised competitive training, and reduce intake modestly in cases of excess adiposity or metabolic risk. When reducing energy, prioritize eliminating discretionary calories rather than cutting core meals. Energy targets should be translated into minimum daily food-group portions to facilitate practical meal planning with Indian foods. As per NIN balanced-diet guidelines, prescribe cereals or millets at each main meal, 2–3 portions of pulses or legumes, 3–5 portions of milk or curd to ensure adequate calcium intake, at least 3 portions of vegetables (including one portion of green leafy vegetables), 1–2 portions of fruits, and daily inclusion of nuts or oilseeds. Visible fats should remain within the recommended range of 20–50 g/day, depending on energy requirements, while avoiding fried snacks for catch-up nutrition. For adolescents with obesity phenotypes, implement a drinks-first strategy by eliminating sugar-sweetened beverages and counseling families to adhere to the WHO free sugar ceiling of less than 10% of total energy intake, ideally below 5%. Practical counseling should include documenting one or two actionable dietary swaps that adolescents can implement in school or hostel settings, with adherence reviewed at subsequent visits.

subtopics.subtopic_title: PER-MEAL PROTEIN TARGETS AND VEGETARIAN PROTEIN QUALITY
subtopics.category: DIET PRESCRIPTION IN CLINIC FOR ADOLESCENT GROWTH AND WEIGHT PHENOTYPES
subtopics.concept: Protein adequacy is a critical factor in supporting healthy weight gain, menstrual regularity, and recovery from physical training in adolescents. For older adolescents, the age-based daily protein requirement is approximately 0.85 g/kg/day, which should ideally be met through food sources. In cases of catch-up growth, chronic illness, or athletic training, protein targets may need to be increased to 1.2–2.0 g/kg/day, depending on the training phase. To optimize muscle protein synthesis and satiety, protein intake should be distributed as approximately 0.25 g/kg per eating occasion (typically 20–40 g) every 3–4 hours across three meals and one to two snacks, rather than concentrating protein consumption in one large evening meal. Practical strategies to achieve these targets in India include incorporating milk or curd at breakfast and bedtime, pulses such as dal, rajma, or chana combined with cereals like rice or roti at lunch and dinner, and paneer, eggs, fish, or lean meat where culturally acceptable. Nuts and oilseeds can serve as convenient snack options. For vegetarian adolescents, it is essential to focus on protein quality by combining cereals with pulses and including dairy or soy to enhance the density of essential amino acids. Additionally, planning for adequate vitamin B12 intake through dairy, eggs, fortified foods, or supplementation is necessary if deficiency is documented. When protein goals are unmet due to appetite or scheduling challenges, portable food-based protein options such as milk tetra packs, curd cups, roasted chana, or egg sandwiches should be prioritized before considering protein powders. Adolescents with obesity should maintain adequate protein and dairy intake while reducing refined cereals, sweetened beverages, and ultra-processed snacks to prevent restrictive dieting that may lead to lean mass loss and rebound eating. Protein distribution should be reassessed in cases of fatigue, recurrent injuries, hair loss, or poor training recovery. Screening for renal or significant hepatic disease is essential before initiating high-protein regimens. These strategies ensure optimal protein intake while addressing specific nutritional needs in paediatric care.

subtopics.subtopic_title: PHENOTYPE-BASED ADD AND SWAP RULES WITH EXECUTABLE DAY PATTERNS
subtopics.category: DIET PRESCRIPTION IN CLINIC FOR ADOLESCENT GROWTH AND WEIGHT PHENOTYPES
subtopics.concept: In the context of adolescent nutrition, phenotype-based add and swap rules with executable day patterns provide a structured, actionable approach to dietary counseling in clinical settings. The framework begins with classifying nutritional status using the WHO BMI-for-age scale, where thinness is defined as a BMI-for-age below -2 SD (severe thinness below -3 SD), and obesity requires staging alongside an assessment of comorbidity risk. Based on the phenotype, specific dietary interventions are prescribed with executable patterns tailored to the adolescent’s needs. For adolescents with thinness requiring catch-up growth without red flags, an additive dietary plan is recommended for four weeks. This includes introducing two energy-dense snacks daily, such as milk or lassi with peanut chikki, banana with peanuts, or curd with cereal. Energy density in meals can be increased by adding 1–2 teaspoons of oil or ghee, provided there is no malabsorption. It is crucial to protect milk/curd and pulses intake targets. A follow-up should be scheduled in 2–4 weeks to assess weight and functional changes. If weight remains static despite adherence, the thinness work-up pathway should be initiated. For adolescents with an obesity or metabolic-risk phenotype, initial interventions focus on reducing discretionary calories without promoting restrictive eating behaviors. Sugar-sweetened beverages and energy drinks should be eliminated, and free sugar intake must remain below WHO recommendations (<10% of energy, ideally <5%). Protein and vegetable portions should be maintained, while refined cereals and fried packaged snacks are reduced. Measurable lifestyle targets, such as 60 minutes of daily physical activity, 8–10 hours of sleep, and zero sugary beverages, should be set, with a review planned in 4–6 weeks. For athletes or those at risk of Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S), breakfast or pre-training fuel and a recovery snack within 1–2 hours post-training are mandatory. Options include milk or curd with fruit or a dal-based snack. Weight cutting and stimulant use are strictly discouraged in minors. For adolescents in hostels, practical add-ons independent of the mess menu, such as milk tetra packs, peanuts, curd cups, bananas, or eggs, should be emphasized.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CBC FERRITIN AND B12 FOLATE DECISION RULES FOR ADOLESCENTS
subtopics.category: LAB INTERPRETATION AND RESOURCE-ADAPTED DECISION RULES
subtopics.concept: In the context of adolescent nutrition, interpreting laboratory results for CBC, ferritin, and vitamin B12/folate requires resource-adapted decision rules to guide management effectively, particularly in settings with limited resources. For microcytic anemia, ferritin is the preferred marker for assessing iron stores; however, as an acute-phase reactant, its reliability is influenced by infection, inflammation, obesity, or chronic disease. A low ferritin level strongly supports iron deficiency, but normal or elevated ferritin levels do not exclude it under these conditions. When available, ferritin should be interpreted alongside an inflammation marker, such as CRP, and the overall CBC findings. If ferritin testing is unavailable, a pragmatic approach involves evaluating microcytosis with high RDW, low reticulocyte response, and dietary or menstrual risk factors. In such cases, an empiric iron trial is recommended, with reassessment planned in 2–4 weeks. A hemoglobin increase of approximately 1–2 g/dL during this interval confirms iron-responsive anemia. If microcytosis is disproportionately severe relative to the anemia, accompanied by a relatively high RBC count or family history, thalassemia trait should be suspected and confirmed through hemoglobin electrophoresis rather than prolonged iron therapy. For macrocytosis, neuropathy, or restrictive vegetarian diets, simultaneous evaluation of vitamin B12 and folate is essential. When both deficiencies are possible, vitamin B12 should be treated first, as folate-only therapy is contraindicated in the presence of neurologic symptoms. In older adolescents without suspected malabsorption, oral high-dose vitamin B12 (commonly 1000 mcg daily) is acceptable if adherence is reliable. For severe deficiency or neurologic involvement, parenteral therapy is preferred, with IM doses of 250–1000 mcg administered on alternate days for 1–2 weeks, followed by 250 mcg weekly until recovery. Transition to oral maintenance therapy is appropriate once the patient stabilizes. These decision rules provide a structured framework for bedside management of common adolescent nutritional deficiencies, ensuring timely and effective interventions.

case_studies.title: Adolescent Nutrition: Anemia Evaluation in a Low-Resource Setting
case_studies.vignette: A 15-year-old girl from a rural area presents to a community health clinic with complaints of fatigue, difficulty concentrating in school, and occasional dizziness for the past two months. She reports heavy menstrual periods lasting 6–7 days and follows a predominantly vegetarian diet due to family preferences. On examination, she appears pale, her heart rate is 96 bpm, and her conjunctivae are mildly pale. Her BMI is 17.5 kg/m². A focused dietary history reveals low intake of iron-rich foods, and she denies consumption of fortified cereals or supplements. A CBC reveals hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL, MCV of 72 fL, and RDW of 16.8%. Ferritin testing is unavailable at the clinic, but the practitioner notes no signs of acute infection or inflammation. The clinician considers whether to initiate an empiric iron trial or refer for additional testing, including ferritin and CRP, at a higher-level facility.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ELECTROLYTE PHOSPHATE MAGNESIUM AND QTC INTERPRETATION IN REFEEDING RISK
subtopics.category: LAB INTERPRETATION AND RESOURCE-ADAPTED DECISION RULES
subtopics.concept: Refeeding syndrome poses a significant risk in adolescents with severe malnutrition, rapid recent weight loss, or prolonged dietary restriction, even when managed with outpatient, meal-based nutritional rehabilitation. The hallmark biochemical abnormality in refeeding syndrome is hypophosphatemia, often accompanied by hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia. These electrolyte disturbances can lead to serious complications, including arrhythmias, muscle weakness, delirium, and respiratory compromise. To mitigate these risks, baseline laboratory tests should include phosphate, magnesium, and glucose levels before increasing caloric intake. Serial monitoring of electrolytes is critical, with reassessments recommended within 24–48 hours of initiating nutritional rehabilitation and continued monitoring during the first several days, as this is the highest-risk period. Hypophosphatemia should be treated urgently following pediatric protocols, and when both potassium and magnesium levels are low, they should be replaced concurrently, as potassium repletion is challenging without adequate magnesium levels. Thiamine supplementation should also be considered in adolescents with markedly restricted intake to address potential coexisting deficiencies that may exacerbate cardiac and neurological risks. Electrocardiogram (ECG) interpretation is an essential component of the safety framework in this context. An ECG should be obtained in the presence of bradycardia, syncope, electrolyte disturbances, or stimulant exposure. Prolonged QTc intervals should be treated as clinically significant, with a practical threshold defined as QTc ≥460 ms in younger adolescents or exceeding sex-specific upper limits in older teens. Prolonged QTc in combination with electrolyte disturbances or ongoing purging behaviors warrants admission-level monitoring rather than routine outpatient follow-up, as these factors significantly increase the risk of life-threatening cardiac events. In summary, effective management of refeeding risk in adolescents requires proactive identification and correction of electrolyte imbalances, vigilant monitoring during the early phases of nutritional rehabilitation, and integration of ECG findings into clinical decision-making to ensure patient safety.

subtopics.subtopic_title: THERAPEUTIC ORAL IRON REGIMENS WITH NON-RESPONSE WORK-UP
subtopics.category: MENSTRUAL HEALTH AND MENARCHE-RELATED NUTRITIONAL RISKS
subtopics.concept: Therapeutic oral iron regimens for anemia in post-menarche adolescents should be tailored based on severity and include structured follow-up to ensure efficacy. Severe anemia, defined as hemoglobin (Hb) <8 g/dL, accompanied by hemodynamic compromise, syncope, or suspected bleeding disorders, warrants urgent referral. Mild to moderate anemia, if stable, can typically be managed outpatient while addressing underlying causes such as dietary restrictions, heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB), parasitic infections, or chronic inflammation. The first-line regimen for mild to moderate anemia involves prescribing two iron-folic acid (IFA) tablets daily for three months, with each tablet containing 60 mg of elemental iron and 500 mcg of folic acid. Patients should be counseled to take the tablets with water or vitamin C-containing fluids to enhance absorption, ensuring they are consumed at least two hours apart from tea, coffee, or calcium. Common side effects like dark stools and constipation should be managed proactively to avoid discontinuation. Hemoglobin levels and symptoms should be reassessed within 2–4 weeks, with an expected Hb rise of 1–2 g/dL if adherence is adequate. Once hemoglobin normalizes, the full course should be completed to replenish iron stores, followed by preventive supplementation through weekly iron and folic acid supplementation (WIFS) and deworming as per National Deworming Day (NDD) schedules when indicated. Non-response to therapy is defined as an Hb increase of less than 1 g/dL after 2–4 weeks of correctly administered treatment. The first step in evaluating non-response is to confirm adherence and proper administration. If adherence is verified, further assessment should focus on ongoing losses or alternative diagnoses, including HMB, occult bleeding, hemoglobinopathies (evidenced by disproportionate microcytosis or elevated RBC count, confirmed via electrophoresis), or pregnancy risk when relevant. Malabsorption or chronic inflammation should also be considered; ferritin levels should be interpreted alongside inflammatory markers like CRP, and screening for celiac disease using tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG-IgA) and total IgA is recommended when clinically indicated. Persistent anemia despite objective adherence necessitates early referral for specialized evaluation and management.

subtopics.subtopic_title: TRIAGE AND FIRST-LINE MANAGEMENT OF HEAVY MENSTRUAL BLEEDING
subtopics.category: MENSTRUAL HEALTH AND MENARCHE-RELATED NUTRITIONAL RISKS
subtopics.concept: The triage and first-line management of heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) in adolescents should follow a structured approach due to the risks of severe iron deficiency anemia and potential underlying bleeding disorders. Admission and escalation criteria begin with assessing hemodynamic stability through pulse, blood pressure, and orthostatic vitals. Patients presenting with hemodynamic instability require immediate emergency management. A detailed history is crucial to identify bleeding disorder risks, including menstrual periods lasting more than seven days with flooding or gushing, bleeding through protection in two hours or less, prior anemia treatment or transfusions, family history of excessive bleeding during surgeries or dental procedures, and symptoms like easy bruising, prolonged epistaxis, or gum bleeding. Physical examination should focus on signs such as pallor, tachycardia, orthostasis, bruising, and thyroid abnormalities, with consideration of hyperandrogenism when relevant. A pelvic examination is not mandatory and should be individualized based on symptoms and safeguarding considerations. Initial laboratory investigations in stable patients should include a complete blood count (CBC) with indices and ferritin if available. Pregnancy testing is indicated when clinically relevant, and targeted coagulation studies, including von Willebrand evaluation, should be performed if the history suggests a bleeding disorder. Hematology input may be required for borderline results. Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) testing is recommended when ovulatory dysfunction or thyroid symptoms are present. Stabilization involves controlling bleeding and correcting anemia simultaneously. First-line medical management includes tranexamic acid at a dose of 15–25 mg/kg (maximum 1 g) administered two to three times daily on heavy bleeding days, and/or NSAIDs taken regularly during menses if not contraindicated. Iron deficiency should be treated to restore hemoglobin levels and replenish iron stores, rather than relying on weekly preventive supplementation. Referral to gynecology or hematology is warranted for suspected bleeding disorders, severe anemia, failure of first-line therapy, or the need for complex hormonal management. Thresholds for admission to the pediatric intensive care unit (PICU) are not specified in the source. Discharge and review triggers are also not specified in the source.

subtopics.subtopic_title: EVALUATION OF MENSTRUAL IRREGULARITY WITH NUTRITION- LINKED DIFFERENTIAL
subtopics.category: MENSTRUAL HEALTH AND MENARCHE-RELATED NUTRITIONAL RISKS
subtopics.concept: The evaluation of menstrual irregularity in adolescents with a nutrition-linked differential requires careful consideration of gynecologic age (years since menarche) and clinical context, treating the menstrual cycle as a vital sign rather than attributing irregularities to “normal puberty.” While pregnancy must first be ruled out when relevant, a structured differential diagnosis for secondary amenorrhea and oligomenorrhea should include thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), hypothalamic suppression due to stress, weight changes, or eating disorders, primary ovarian insufficiency, and, selectively, anatomic causes. Two nutrition-linked pathways warrant active case-finding. First, low energy availability combined with training stress can suppress the hypothalamic–pituitary–ovarian axis, even in adolescents with a normal BMI. This condition, termed relative energy deficiency in sport (RED-S), should be managed by increasing energy availability, temporarily reducing training load, ensuring adequate calcium and vitamin D intake, and assessing fracture risk. Second, obesity with hyperandrogenism may indicate adolescent PCOS, which requires both persistent menstrual irregularity (defined by time post-menarche) and clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism for diagnosis after excluding mimics. Notably, ovarian ultrasound morphology is not diagnostic within eight years post-menarche. Management strategies must align with the identified pathway. In suspected RED-S, addressing underlying nutritional deficits is critical, and treatment should avoid focusing solely on downstream symptoms, such as prescribing hormones without concurrent nutrition rehabilitation. For suspected PCOS in adolescents with overweight or obesity, interventions should prioritize sustainable improvements in dietary quality, daily physical activity, regular sleep patterns, and screening for metabolic comorbidities in accordance with the Indian Academy of Pediatrics (IAP) obesity guidelines. These tailored approaches emphasize the importance of addressing the root causes of menstrual irregularities rather than merely alleviating symptoms, ensuring comprehensive care for adolescents with nutrition-linked menstrual health concerns.

subtopics.subtopic_title: VITAMIN D AND CALCIUM DEFICIENCY DIAGNOSIS AND TREATMENT
subtopics.concept: Adolescence represents a critical window for achieving peak bone mass, making the diagnosis and management of vitamin D and calcium deficiency particularly important in this age group. In India, low habitual calcium intake and vitamin D deficiency are widely prevalent, as highlighted by the CNNS survey, which identifies a substantial minority of adolescents affected. Clinically, bone pain, stress injuries, and delayed growth should prompt a syndromic evaluation rather than attributing symptoms solely to vitamin D deficiency. This evaluation should include consideration of anemia risk, undernutrition, and restrictive eating patterns such as RED-S. Laboratory testing for 25(OH)D levels is recommended in cases where symptoms, fracture risk, very low sun exposure, or markedly low calcium diets suggest a high likelihood of deficiency. Once confirmed, treatment should align with pediatric guidelines, ensuring concurrent calcium supplementation to address deficiency. The Indian Academy of Pediatrics (IAP) guidelines provide adolescent-specific treatment protocols for vitamin D deficiency and nutritional rickets. These include daily cholecalciferol dosing of approximately 3000–6000 IU/day for children aged 1–18 years or an intermittent regimen such as 60,000 IU weekly for six weeks, alongside calcium supplementation at doses of 500–800 mg/day. Maintenance therapy should follow initial treatment, with efforts to ensure adequate dietary calcium and safe sunlight exposure. For adolescents without confirmed deficiency, the focus should remain on meeting daily requirements through diet and sunlight, with maintenance supplementation only when intake or sun exposure is predictably inadequate. It is crucial to avoid repeated unsupervised high-dose vitamin D sachets, as toxicity can occur, particularly with repeated “stoss” regimens without reassessment. Emphasis should be placed on food-first calcium sources, including milk, curd, paneer, ragi, millets, and fortified foods where available. Regular reassessment of symptoms and functional outcomes, alongside biochemical recovery, is essential to ensure effective management and prevent complications.

case_studies.title: Adolescent with Bone Pain and Fatigue
case_studies.vignette: A 14-year-old girl from a rural area in India presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of generalized bone pain, fatigue, and difficulty climbing stairs for the past three months. Her mother reports that she has had no recent illnesses or injuries but mentions that the girl avoids outdoor activities due to the heat and spends most of her time indoors studying. Her diet primarily consists of rice and lentils, with minimal intake of milk or dairy products. On examination, she appears thin with a BMI at the 10th percentile. She has tenderness over her lower legs and mild muscle weakness in her proximal lower extremities. There is no visible deformity or swelling. Laboratory tests reveal a serum 25(OH)D level of 10 ng/mL (normal: >20 ng/mL) and a serum calcium level of 8.3 mg/dL (normal: 8.5–10.5 mg/dL).
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the clinical presentation and diagnostic criteria for vitamin D and calcium deficiency in adolescents, particularly in the context of low dietary intake and limited sun exposure.

case_studies.title: Adolescent with Bone Pain and Fatigue in a Low-Resource Setting
case_studies.vignette: A 14-year-old girl from a rural village in India presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of diffuse bone pain and fatigue for the past 3 months. She reports difficulty concentrating in school and occasional leg cramps at night. Her mother notes that she has been less active recently and avoids outdoor activities due to the intense heat. On further questioning, her diet is found to be predominantly cereal-based with minimal dairy or green leafy vegetables. She has no history of fractures or chronic illness. subtopics.subtopic_title: FOLATE DEFICIENCY TREATMENT AND B12 SAFETY PRECAUTIONS
subtopics.concept: Folate deficiency in adolescents should be treated when low folate levels are identified alongside macrocytosis or a dietary history suggestive of inadequate intake of folate-rich foods. However, it is critical to first exclude or concurrently treat vitamin B12 deficiency, as folate supplementation can correct anemia while allowing B12-related neurological damage to progress unchecked. Folate deficiency should be considered in adolescents presenting with macrocytosis, fatigue, poor growth, or a history of restrictive eating patterns or diets low in pulses and green leafy vegetables. The first-line treatment is folic acid 5 mg orally once daily for approximately four months, followed by reassessment of dietary intake and risk factors. For adolescents with persistent risk factors such as chronic low intake, malabsorption, or recurrent restrictive dieting, a maintenance regimen of 5 mg folic acid every 1–7 days may be implemented under supervision, alongside dietetic counseling. In some cases, 5–10 mg daily may be used for adolescents aged 12–18 years at sustained high risk, but dosing should adhere to local protocols and the specific diagnosis. Monitoring should be tailored to the clinical context rather than focusing solely on laboratory values. A repeat complete blood count (CBC) is recommended to document hematologic response and evaluate symptom resolution, particularly in cases of multifactorial anemia involving iron, folate, and B12 deficiencies. If the adolescent is enrolled in an iron–folic acid supplementation program, such as the Weekly Iron and Folic Acid Supplementation (WIFS) program, it is important to clarify whether the program’s dose is preventive or therapeutic to avoid duplicative or unsupervised supplementation. The ultimate goal of treatment is dietary restoration, emphasizing regular consumption of folate-rich foods such as green leafy vegetables, legumes, and citrus fruits, along with improved overall energy adequacy. Adverse effects and additional safety precautions related to folate supplementation or B12 deficiency management were not specified in the source.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ZINC DEFICIENCY THERAPY AND COPPER DEFICIENCY RISK MITIGATION
subtopics.concept: Zinc deficiency should be considered in adolescents presenting with restrictive diets, chronic diarrhea or malabsorption, recurrent infections, poor wound healing, dermatitis, or poor growth, particularly after ruling out more common causes. In India, zinc inadequacy often coexists with deficiencies in iron and vitamin B12/folate, especially in cases of low dietary quality, necessitating a “cluster” approach to diagnosis and management. When clinical suspicion of zinc deficiency is reasonable, therapy may be initiated with a clear plan for reassessment. For subclinical zinc deficiency, the recommended outpatient regimen is elemental zinc at a dose of 1 mg/kg/day administered orally in 1–3 divided doses, with clinical reassessment and, if feasible, repeat zinc levels after approximately one month. Severe or inherited conditions such as acrodermatitis enteropathica require higher doses, around 3 mg/kg/day, and should involve specialist comanagement. Supplementation should continue until zinc levels and symptoms normalize, and dietary risk factors are addressed, after which a food-first maintenance strategy should be adopted. Open-ended use of zinc tonics is discouraged. Chronic high-dose zinc supplementation can impair copper absorption, leading to copper deficiency, which may manifest as anemia, neutropenia, or neurological symptoms. Families should be counseled to strictly adhere to prescribed doses, and copper evaluation should be considered if prolonged high-dose zinc therapy is used or if unexplained cytopenias occur. To mitigate copper deficiency risks, it is crucial to avoid exceeding recommended zinc doses. Food-based strategies for zinc repletion and maintenance should emphasize Indian dietary staples such as eggs, dairy (if acceptable), dals and legumes (e.g., chana, rajma), nuts, seeds, and whole grains. Families should be educated on the impact of high-phytate, cereal-heavy diets in reducing zinc absorption and the importance of dietary variety and proper food preparation practices. Not specified in source are alternative regimens for zinc allergy or intolerance, specific protocols for multidrug-resistant/extensively drug-resistant cases, and detailed adverse effect monitoring guidelines.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CLINICAL EVALUATION AND FOLLOW-UP PLAN FOR ADOLESCENT THINNESS
subtopics.concept: The clinical evaluation and follow-up plan for adolescent thinness requires a structured approach to identify underlying causes and guide management. Thinness or short stature in adolescence should prompt a thorough classification of causes rather than immediate prescription of appetite stimulants. It is crucial to use growth trajectory and pubertal context to differentiate constitutional thinness, characterized by normal height velocity and pubertal progression, from chronic undernutrition, systemic diseases, or disordered eating. Key red flags include crossing down of weight centiles, slowed height velocity, delayed or arrested pubertal progression, chronic diarrhea, chronic cough or fever (including tuberculosis risk), and restrictive eating or excessive exercise. Indian data highlight the long-term consequences of adolescent undernutrition, including fatigue, poor school performance, and risks of adverse outcomes when undernourished girls enter pregnancy. Although pregnancy-specific concerns are not relevant here, these findings underscore the critical need for early intervention. In clinical practice, undernutrition often coexists with micronutrient deficiencies and gaps in program delivery. A detailed history should include assessment of meal regularity, access to school meals, adherence to weekly iron and folic acid supplementation (WIFS), deworming history, and food insecurity or migration patterns that may disrupt dietary intake. Fatigue or pallor warrants screening for anemia using a complete blood count (CBC), and atypical indices should prompt consideration of overlapping iron, vitamin B12, and folate deficiencies. Management must address medical contributors such as anemia, helminth infections, or chronic infections while simultaneously restoring structured meals. A balanced diet should include breakfast, a daily protein source, and a daily calcium source, emphasizing energy-dense but nutrient-dense foods rather than ultra-processed snacks. Supplements should not replace meals. Weight trajectory and functional outcomes should be reassessed in 4–6 weeks. Persistent lack of catch-up growth, ongoing weight loss, or stalled pubertal progression necessitates further evaluation or referral to a specialist.

subtopics.subtopic_title: OBESITY TREATMENT STAGING AND ESCALATION TO SPECIALIST CARE
subtopics.concept: Obesity treatment in adolescents should follow a structured, staged approach with clear decision points for escalation, guided by BMI-for-age staging and comorbidity risk. Admission and escalation criteria involve assessing BMI thresholds and associated risks. Adolescents aged 10 years and older with obesity require an initial outpatient evaluation, including blood pressure measurement, waist-related risk markers (such as acanthosis and hepatomegaly), and comorbidity screening per IAP guidelines. This screening includes lipid profiles, glycemic status, and liver enzymes. The initial plan should focus on setting 2–3 measurable targets for behavior modification over the next month, such as achieving 60 minutes of moderate-to-vigorous physical activity daily, ensuring 8–10 hours of sleep per night, and eliminating sugar-sweetened beverages. Dietary recommendations include replacing packaged snacks with healthier options like fruits, roasted chana, or curd/buttermilk and maintaining regular meals to prevent restrictive–binge eating cycles. Progress should be reviewed in 4–6 weeks using objective markers like activity days per week, sugary drinks consumed per week, and sleep duration, while addressing barriers such as school or hostel constraints. Escalation to specialist care is anchored on the severity of obesity. Class 2 obesity is defined as a BMI ≥120% to <140% of the 95th percentile or BMI ≥35 to <40 kg/m², whichever is lower, while class 3 obesity is defined as a BMI ≥140% of the 95th percentile or BMI ≥40 kg/m², whichever is lower. Pharmacotherapy may be considered for adolescents aged 12 years or older with class 2 obesity and major comorbidities or class 3 obesity, but only as an adjunct to comprehensive lifestyle therapy and under specialist monitoring. Bariatric or metabolic surgery may be indicated for adolescents older than 12 years with class 2 obesity and significant comorbidities or class 3 obesity after at least six months of unsuccessful intensive lifestyle modification and pharmacotherapy. Such procedures should be performed in centers with multidisciplinary pediatric capabilities, and in India, affordability and continuity of follow-up must be confirmed before escalation. Thresholds for PICU care and discharge and review triggers were not specified in the source.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MACRONUTRIENT TARGETS AND MEAL TIMING FOR ADOLESCENT ATHLETES
subtopics.category: SPORTS NUTRITION AND RELATIVE ENERGY DEFICIENCY IN SPORT
subtopics.concept: Macronutrient targets and meal timing for adolescent athletes should focus on supporting both growth and training demands. A structured meal plan comprising breakfast, lunch, and dinner, supplemented with planned training snacks, is essential. Macronutrient targets must be individualized based on the athlete's training intensity, growth trajectory, and performance goals. Carbohydrate requirements can be periodized according to training load: 3–5 g/kg/day for light skill-based training, 5–7 g/kg/day for moderate training (~1 hour/day), 6–10 g/kg/day for higher endurance loads (1–3 hours/day), and 8–12 g/kg/day for extreme commitments (>4–5 hours/day). Before sessions lasting over 60 minutes, a pre-exercise carbohydrate intake of 1–4 g/kg, consumed 1–4 hours prior, is recommended, avoiding meals high in fat or fiber if gastrointestinal discomfort arises. During prolonged exercise, 30–60 g of carbohydrate per hour is advised for activities lasting 1–2.5 hours, with highly trained athletes potentially tolerating up to ~90 g/hour for events exceeding 2.5–3 hours, provided this is practiced beforehand. For recovery, particularly when less than 8 hours separate intense sessions, early refueling should target 1–1.2 g/kg/hour of carbohydrate during the first 4 hours, followed by a return to daily carbohydrate targets. Protein needs for repair and adaptation range from 1.2–2.0 g/kg/day, with intake distributed across the day. This includes ~0.3 g/kg immediately after key training sessions and every 3–5 hours across meals. Practical sources of protein for Indian adolescent athletes include dal–rice or roti with curd, rajma, chole, paneer, tofu, eggs, fish, chicken (where culturally acceptable), and milk-based snacks, which can meet nutritional needs without reliance on protein powders in most cases. Sports-specific foods may serve as substitutes during long events but should not replace regular meals, as proper nutrition is critical for growth in adolescent athletes.

subtopics.subtopic_title: GROWTH BONE HEALTH AND MICRONUTRIENT PRIORITIES IN ADOLESCENT ATHLETES
subtopics.category: SPORTS NUTRITION AND RELATIVE ENERGY DEFICIENCY IN SPORT
subtopics.concept: Adolescent athletes have unique nutritional needs due to the simultaneous demands of growth, puberty, and bone mineral accrual, which must take precedence over performance optimization. Ensuring adequate energy availability is the foundational priority before addressing macronutrient or performance-specific needs. Key micronutrients influencing growth and bone health include calcium, vitamin D, and iron. Daily calcium intake should be prioritized through sources such as milk, curd, ragi, or fortified alternatives, alongside feasible sunlight exposure to support vitamin D synthesis. In cases of documented vitamin D deficiency, treatment should follow established pediatric protocols rather than indefinite low-dose supplementation. Iron status warrants early screening in menstruating, endurance, or vegetarian athletes presenting with fatigue, recurrent injuries, or menstrual irregularities. Ferritin levels must be interpreted cautiously, as inflammation or obesity can falsely elevate ferritin despite underlying functional iron deficiency. Vegetarian athletes require intentional dietary planning to ensure adequate protein quality and vitamin B12 sufficiency. Cereal–pulse combinations combined with dairy or soy are recommended, and proven B12 deficiency should be managed with therapeutic replacement rather than reliance on tonics. In resource-limited settings, low-cost, nutrient-dense options such as bananas, poha, rice, dal, and milk should be emphasized over commercial sports products. Clinical red flags such as recurrent stress injuries, delayed recovery, falling weight trajectory, secondary amenorrhea or oligomenorrhea, and bradycardia should prompt evaluation for Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S). Management in such cases includes mandatory breakfast and post-training recovery meals, a reduction in training load until clinical stability is achieved, and strict avoidance of weight-cutting advice in minors. These strategies underscore the importance of safeguarding growth, bone health, and overall development in adolescent athletes while addressing their specific micronutrient and energy needs.

case_studies.title: Adolescent Athlete with Fatigue and Recurrent Injuries
case_studies.vignette: A 15-year-old female state-level badminton player presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of fatigue, recurrent shin pain, and difficulty recovering from matches over the past three months. She reports no significant weight loss but mentions irregular menstrual cycles over the past six months. Her diet is predominantly vegetarian, consisting mainly of rice, dal, and vegetables, with occasional milk intake. She trains for 3–4 hours daily and admits skipping breakfast frequently due to early morning practice sessions. On examination, her BMI is at the 25th percentile, and there is mild tenderness over the anterior tibia bilaterally without swelling. There is no pallor, and her vitals are stable. A dietary recall reveals inadequate calcium and protein intake. Her mother expresses concern about her declining performance and increasing injuries despite her rigorous training schedule.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the importance of addressing energy availability, micronutrient deficiencies (calcium, vitamin D, iron), and menstrual irregularities in adolescent athletes, and identify red flags for Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S).

subtopics.subtopic_title: HYDRATION PLANNING AND HEAT ILLNESS PREVENTION DECISION POINTS
subtopics.category: SPORTS NUTRITION AND RELATIVE ENERGY DEFICIENCY IN SPORT
subtopics.concept:
Hydration planning and heat illness prevention in adolescent athletes requires careful attention to objective measures, as thirst is an unreliable indicator during high-intensity training or heat exposure. A practical approach is to monitor body mass changes before and after practice or matches, aiming to prevent body mass loss exceeding 2%. Adolescents should begin sessions euhydrated, with light-colored morning urine as a visual cue, or, when available, urine specific gravity ≤1.020 measured via refractometer as a reliable threshold. During training, unrestricted access to fluids should be ensured, with sodium supplementation recommended in cases of heavy sweating to enhance fluid retention and mitigate cumulative dehydration. In Indian settings, oral rehydration solution (ORS) packets or salted foods and fluids are pragmatic options during prolonged heat exposure, as plain water alone may be insufficient for high sweat losses. Energy drinks with high caffeine content are unsuitable for hydration in adolescents and should be avoided.

Recognizing and managing heat illness is critical for adolescent athletes, and families and coaches should be educated on its signs and emergency response. Exertional heat stroke, a medical emergency, is indicated by collapse or neuropsychiatric impairment during exertion, accompanied by a markedly elevated core temperature, often exceeding 40.5°C. Immediate cooling and urgent referral are essential in such cases. Any exertional collapse with altered sensorium should be treated as heat stroke until proven otherwise. Weight-category and leanness-focused sports pose additional risks, as practices like rapid dehydration, aggressive weight cutting, fluid restriction, sauna use, or diuretics/laxatives can precipitate heat illness and collapse. Adolescents engaging in these practices should be counseled against them, and such behaviors should be treated as safeguarding concerns.

This framework emphasizes the importance of proactive hydration strategies, sodium supplementation, and vigilant monitoring for heat illness symptoms, ensuring the safety and well-being of adolescent athletes during training and competition.

case_studies.vignette: A 16-year-old male athlete from a rural school in India presents to the clinic after collapsing during an afternoon football practice session in the summer heat. The coach reports the adolescent had been training intensively for over two hours with limited fluid intake. He complained of headache, dizziness, and nausea before the collapse. On arrival, the boy is lethargic and disoriented. His skin is flushed, hot to touch, and dry, with no visible sweating. subtopics.subtopic_title: RECOGNITION AND INITIAL MANAGEMENT OF RELATIVE ENERGY DEFICIENCY IN SPORT
subtopics.category: SPORTS NUTRITION AND RELATIVE ENERGY DEFICIENCY IN SPORT
subtopics.concept: Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S) refers to multisystem dysfunction caused by low energy availability and can affect both male and female adolescents, including those with a normal BMI. Adolescents are particularly vulnerable due to the overlap of low energy availability with critical developmental milestones such as peak height velocity, menarche, and peak bone mass accrual. Active screening for RED-S is essential in athletes presenting with performance decline, recurrent illness, stress fractures, persistent bone pain, mood changes, disordered eating behaviors, or menstrual dysfunction in girls. Menstrual dysfunction in female athletes should always be treated as a systemic warning sign rather than dismissed as a normal consequence of training. A thorough history should include an assessment of training volume, recent increases in load, heat exposure, and any external pressures to lose weight. Initial management focuses on increasing energy availability through structured meals and snacks, distributing protein intake throughout the day, and ensuring adequate calcium and vitamin D levels. Temporary reductions in training load, including cessation of additional conditioning, are necessary until recovery markers improve. Anemia and other micronutrient deficiencies that exacerbate fatigue and impair physiological adaptation must be evaluated and treated. The use of combined oral contraceptives solely to address amenorrhea is discouraged, as this may mask persistent low energy availability and ongoing bone health risks. In weight-category sports, explicit prevention counseling is critical. Adolescents should be advised against dehydration-driven weight manipulation and rapid weight loss. Objective monitoring of growth trajectories, vital signs, injury patterns, and menstrual function is essential. Persistent red flags warrant early referral to sports medicine, endocrinology, or adolescent medicine specialists for further evaluation and management.

subtopics.subtopic_title: OUTPATIENT SCREENING TOOLS AND MEDICAL RISK STRATIFICATION
subtopics.concept: Outpatient screening tools and medical risk stratification are essential in identifying eating disorders in adolescents, particularly when clinical red flags such as a falling growth trajectory, rapid weight change, amenorrhea or oligomenorrhea, syncope or dizziness, bradycardia, chronic abdominal complaints with restrictive eating patterns, overtraining, or caregiver concerns about eating behaviors are present. Screening should begin with a brief tool to raise suspicion, followed by a clinician interview for further evaluation. The SCOFF questionnaire, a five-item screener, is commonly used, with two or more “yes” responses serving as the threshold for further assessment. Another widely utilized tool is the EAT-26, where a total score of 20 or higher traditionally indicates the need for professional evaluation. However, it is important to note that low scores on these tools do not exclude the possibility of an eating disorder, and their performance may vary across different populations. A positive screening result should prompt immediate medical risk assessment. This includes measuring resting heart rate, temperature, and orthostatic vital signs, as well as interpreting weight changes in the context of a trajectory rather than isolated values. If restriction, purging, or physiological symptoms are present, a baseline medical panel should be ordered. This panel includes a complete blood count (CBC), electrolytes (including magnesium and phosphate), glucose, liver function tests (LFTs), urinalysis, and thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), with additional micronutrient levels such as iron, vitamin D, vitamin B12, and zinc guided by the adolescent’s diet history. An electrocardiogram (ECG) is recommended in cases of significant weight loss, bradycardia, orthostatic instability, electrolyte abnormalities, palpitations, syncope, or exposure to stimulant supplements. It is critical to recognize that normal laboratory results do not exclude serious illness. Clinical decisions should prioritize signs of vital sign instability, ongoing weight loss, and the inability of the family to implement supervised meals, as these factors indicate higher medical risk.

subtopics.subtopic_title: FAMILY-SUPPORTED OUTPATIENT MANAGEMENT WITH ESCALATION RULES
subtopics.concept: Adolescents with eating disorders can often be managed as outpatients if they are medically stable, provided there is structured monitoring and active family involvement. Family-based treatment is the first-line approach for managing anorexia nervosa in this population, emphasizing parental or caregiver supervision of meals and snacks, minimizing negotiation around food intake, and prioritizing weight restoration alongside medical stability. As part of initial management, competitive sports and excessive exercise should be discontinued until vital signs and weight trajectory stabilize, particularly when exercise is used as a compensatory behavior. A comprehensive baseline medical evaluation is essential and should include a complete blood count (CBC), electrolytes (with phosphate and magnesium), glucose, liver function tests (LFT), and an electrocardiogram (ECG) if clinically indicated, to promptly identify early signs of deterioration. A structured meal plan, consisting of three meals and snacks, should be established, and follow-up should occur at short intervals, with weekly reviews being the minimum during the early phase of restriction. These frequent reviews should continue until there is evidence of consistent weight gain and resolution of orthostatic symptoms, after which visits can be spaced further apart. Families should be educated to recognize specific warning signs that necessitate escalation of care. These include ongoing weight loss, new episodes of syncope, worsening orthostatic symptoms, vomiting or purging, renewed food refusal or food hiding, significant reduction in urine output, suicidal ideation, or an unsafe home environment that impedes supervised feeding. In settings where dedicated eating disorder teams are unavailable, families should be directed to the nearest adolescent physician or pediatrician, psychiatrist, and dietitian, with telehealth options considered when travel is a barrier. To ensure cohesive care, a shared monitoring template should be used, documenting weights under standardized conditions, orthostatic vitals, and adherence to the meal plan. This approach minimizes fragmented care and supports effective outpatient management. Discharge and review triggers were not specified in the source.

case_studies.title: Adolescent with Weight Loss and Fatigue: Outpatient Monitoring Challenges
case_studies.vignette: A 15-year-old girl presents to a community health clinic in an urban Indian setting with her mother, reporting progressive weight loss over the past 3 months, accompanied by fatigue and decreased concentration in school. The mother mentions that the patient has been avoiding family meals, citing 'feeling full' or 'not hungry,' and has recently started exercising excessively, often skipping meals afterward. On focused examination, her weight is at the 5th percentile (a drop from the 25th percentile six months ago), with a BMI of 15.5 kg/m². Vital signs reveal a resting heart rate of 56 bpm, blood pressure of 90/60 mmHg, and a positive orthostatic change in heart rate (+25 bpm on standing). She denies vomiting or laxative use but admits to feeling lightheaded occasionally. Baseline investigations, including CBC and electrolytes, are within normal limits. The family is motivated to support her recovery but expresses concern about their ability to monitor her consistently at home. They inquire about signs that would require urgent medical attention.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the thresholds for outpatient management versus escalation in adolescents with eating disorders, emphasizing the role of family support and structured monitoring in maintaining medical stability.

case_studies.title: Adolescent Nutrition: Family-Supported Outpatient Management with Escalation Rules
case_studies.vignette: A 15-year-old girl is brought to the outpatient clinic by her parents, who are concerned about her significant weight loss over the past 3 months. She has become increasingly restrictive with her eating, avoiding meals with the family and citing a desire to 'eat healthy' or 'cut carbs.' Her parents report that she spends long hours exercising in her room and has recently started skipping school due to fatigue. Over the last week, she has complained of dizziness when standing and has had two episodes of near-syncope. On examination, she appears thin, with a BMI below the 3rd percentile for her age. Her pulse is 56 bpm, and her blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg, dropping to 78/55 mmHg on standing. Capillary refill is 3 seconds. Initial laboratory tests reveal mild hypokalemia (3.2 mmol/L) and a normal glucose level. The family is motivated to participate in her care but expresses concern about her escalating behaviors and their ability to manage her at home.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess outpatient versus inpatient management for adolescents with eating disorders, focusing on medical stability and family readiness, and identify escalation triggers requiring higher-level care.

subtopics.subtopic_title: OUTPATIENT FIRST-WEEK PLAN FOR ANOREXIA NERVOSA AND ATYPICAL ANOREXIA
subtopics.concept: In the outpatient management of adolescents with anorexia nervosa (AN) or atypical anorexia nervosa (atypical AN) during the first week, a structured and detailed plan is essential if the patient is medically stable for outpatient care. On Day 0, a standardized weight should be recorded using the same scale, with the adolescent in light clothing and post-void. Orthostatic vitals must be measured after 5 minutes supine and 3 minutes standing. Baseline laboratory tests, including electrolytes (with phosphate and magnesium), glucose, and liver function tests (LFTs), should be ordered. An electrocardiogram (ECG) is indicated if bradycardia, orthostasis, electrolyte abnormalities, palpitations, syncope, or stimulant exposure is present. Immediate initiation of parent-led meal supervision is critical, with caregivers plating three meals and 2–3 snacks daily to relieve the adolescent of portioning decisions. Energy density should be increased by incorporating milk, curd, oils, and energy-dense snacks, avoiding calorie counting in front of the adolescent. Competitive sports and compensatory exercise must be discontinued until weight trajectory and vital signs stabilize. Monitoring frequency should be clearly defined. During the first week, twice-weekly medical reviews are recommended for patients with low weight, borderline vital signs, or unreliable food intake. Weekly reviews are the minimum once early weight restoration begins. Each visit should follow a consistent template, including weight measurement, orthostatic vitals, symptom assessment, and evaluation of adherence to meal supervision, to ensure cohesive care. Escalation to same-day admission is warranted if any of the following criteria are met: weight ≤75% of median BMI, dehydration, electrolyte disturbances (particularly hypophosphatemia), prolonged QTc, severe bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, significant orthostatic changes (pulse increase >20/min or blood pressure drop >20/10 mmHg), syncope, seizures, acute food refusal, uncontrollable binge/purge behavior, or unsafe psychiatric comorbidities. This structured approach ensures comprehensive outpatient management during the critical first week, allowing for timely intervention and minimizing risks associated with medical instability.

subtopics.subtopic_title: ARFID PHENOTYPE- BASED TRIAGE AND MULTIDISCIPLINARY MANAGEMENT
subtopics.concept: Avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder (ARFID) is characterised by restrictive eating that leads to weight loss, faltering growth, nutritional deficiencies, reliance on supplements, or psychosocial impairment, without the body-image–driven fear seen in other eating disorders. It often manifests as extreme picky eating, fear of choking or vomiting following a triggering event, or a general lack of interest in food. Clinicians must triage ARFID cases based on the dominant phenotype, as management strategies differ. Sensory-based avoidance requires gradual and repeated exposure to textures and smells, often facilitated by occupational or feeding therapy. Fear-based avoidance, typically following choking, vomiting, or abdominal pain, necessitates evaluation of the underlying medical condition, followed by structured exposure therapy combined with anxiety management. Low-interest presentations benefit from scheduled meals, energy-dense foods, and routines that support appetite, such as optimising sleep and activity levels, while screening for depression and neurodevelopmental conditions. Initial medical assessment should follow protocols for restrictive eating disorders, including plotting growth trajectories, evaluating for micronutrient deficiencies, and ordering targeted laboratory tests for nutrients such as iron, vitamin D, vitamin B12, and zinc when intake is restricted. Broad panels are discouraged. Oral nutrition supplements may serve as a temporary bridge when dietary expansion is slow, but they should not replace efforts to increase food intake through exposure strategies. Escalation to higher-level care is warranted when medical instability thresholds are met, including bradycardia, hypotension, orthostatic changes exceeding a 20-pulse increase or a >20/10 mmHg blood pressure drop, or electrolyte disturbances. Escalation is also necessary when families are unable to implement structured feeding plans. In India, multidisciplinary care for ARFID may require assembling a team comprising a paediatrician, dietitian, mental health professional, and, when relevant, ENT or gastroenterology specialists, often with telehealth support. Discharge and review triggers were not specified in the source.

subtopics.subtopic_title: HOSPITALIZATION THRESHOLDS AND INPATIENT MONITORING FOR MEDICAL INSTABILITY
subtopics.concept: Hospitalization for adolescents with suspected eating disorders is warranted when physiologic instability or severe malnutrition renders outpatient care unsafe. Specific thresholds for admission include a body mass index (BMI) at or below 75% of the median for age and sex, dehydration, electrolyte disturbances, or electrocardiogram (ECG) abnormalities such as prolonged QTc interval or severe bradycardia. Physiologic instability is defined by a heart rate of less than 50 beats per minute during the day or less than 45 beats per minute at night, blood pressure at or below 90/45 mm Hg, body temperature below 35.6°C, or significant orthostatic changes. Orthostatic criteria include a pulse increase of more than 20 beats per minute, with an increase of more than 40 beats per minute being particularly concerning, or a drop in systolic blood pressure of more than 20 mm Hg or diastolic blood pressure of more than 10 mm Hg. Additional indications for admission include acute food refusal, uncontrollable bingeing or purging, syncope, seizures, arrested growth or development, failure of structured outpatient treatment, or the presence of psychiatric comorbidities that compromise safety. Inpatient monitoring should follow a structured protocol. This includes serial vital signs with orthostatic measurements, daily weights taken under standardized conditions, and regular monitoring of electrolytes such as phosphate and magnesium, particularly in cases where refeeding syndrome is a concern. An ECG should be obtained if bradycardia or QTc abnormalities are identified. The risk of refeeding syndrome is primarily influenced by the degree of malnutrition and recent weight loss, necessitating close monitoring during the initial days of nutritional rehabilitation. Electrolyte abnormalities should be corrected according to pediatric protocols. Current practices often favor higher-calorie, meal-based refeeding approaches with vigilant monitoring, as opposed to very low-calorie starts. However, decisions regarding caloric escalation should be individualized and guided by an experienced multidisciplinary team to ensure safety and efficacy.
